Safety Mar 28, 2026

Not Again: "Strong Odor" Evacuates DC Air Traffic Control For 2nd Time This Month

A similar incident recorded two weeks ago was caused by an overheating circuit board, which had to be replaced.

Recurring facility evacuations from equipment failures raise urgent questions about maintenance protocols and redundancy measures at critical infrastructure sites.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 28, 2026

Is It True That Engine Chevrons Pose A Problem To Performance?

Engine chevrons have become a defining feature of modern aircraft, but do they really pose a problem for performance?

Chevrons reduce jet noise by 3-4 decibels through shock cell disruption, with minimal fuel burn penalties that are offset by noise compliance benefits in noise-restricted airspace.

See Simple Flying →

Drones Mar 28, 2026

Robinson Unmanned

Robinson Unmanned (formerly Ascent AeroSystems) is the uncrewed aircraft systems (UAS) business unit of Robinson...

Robinson's pivot to UAS operations positions the established rotorcraft manufacturer to capture growing demand for unmanned vertical lift across commercial and industrial sectors.

See sUAS News →

Drones Mar 28, 2026

Quantum Systems Strengthens Ukraine’s Air Defence Capabilities with 15,000 Interceptor Contract and WIY Drones Investment

In alignment with the defence priorities set out by the Ukrainian Government, Quantum Systems has...

Commercial drone manufacturer pivoting to defense production signals how civilian UAS companies are becoming critical military suppliers during active conflicts.

See sUAS News →

Military Mar 28, 2026

6 Reasons Why The F-35 Is So Expensive To Operate

The F-35 is expensive to operate because it is the most capable fighter jet that requires a specialized ecosystem greater than other fighter jets.

F-35's operational costs exceed other fighters due to its integrated logistics ecosystem, not just aircraft complexity, fundamentally changing how military aviation budgets must account for platform support infrastructure.

See Simple Flying →

PAI
Safety Mar 28, 2026

Safety Culture in Aviation SMS

How organizational culture shapes safety outcomes and what it takes to build a reporting culture that supports SMS.

Organizations that psychologically safe reporting environments see SMS effectiveness multiply, as frontline workers freely escalate hazards before incidents occur.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Safety Mar 28, 2026

“Today We Mourn”: Pilots Of Air Canada Flight 8646 Honored As Bodies Return Home

The return of Captain Antoine Forest and Co-pilot Mackenzie Gunther's remains to Montreal and Ottawa were met with tributes at the airport.

The aviation community's formal ceremonial response to pilot fatalities reinforces the profession's deep culture of mutual respect and collective grieving among flight crews.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 28, 2026

Why Airlines Retire Aircraft Early

Airlines are retiring young aircraft like the A320neo and A220, primarily due to engine reliability issues and skyrocketing engine values.

Engine supply chain constraints and elevated component values are forcing airlines to scrap relatively new aircraft rather than wait for repairs, creating unprecedented economic pressure on fleet management.

See Simple Flying →

Regulatory Mar 28, 2026

Singapore delays SAF rollout levy amid Middle East conflict

As the war in Iran continues to roil world oil markets, Singapore has delayed the rollout of its…...

Geopolitical disruptions to fuel markets are forcing governments to recalibrate aviation sustainability timelines, signaling that climate-focused levies may be vulnerable to energy price volatility.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Mar 28, 2026

Artemis 2 astronauts arrive at KSC

The four Artemis 2 astronauts arrived in Florida March 27 for final preparations ahead of a launch still scheduled as soon as April 1.

The arrival of Artemis 2 crew signals NASA's imminent return to lunar missions after a decade-long gap, with commercial aerospace suppliers now critical to deep-space exploration success.

See SpaceNews →

Regulatory Mar 28, 2026

SMS Declaration of Compliance

What the SMS Declaration of Compliance means for operators and what the FAA expects.

The SMS Declaration of Compliance standardizes safety management expectations across operator types, creating measurable compliance benchmarks beyond traditional regulatory checklists.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Safety Mar 28, 2026

What Happens When A Pilot Rejects Landing At The Last Second

A last-second rejected landing demands precise aircraft handling and coordination between both pilots and air traffic control.

Go-around procedures require immediate coordination with ATC to secure priority handling and alternate runway assignment within seconds to prevent traffic conflicts.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 27, 2026

The 1 Metric That Determines Airline Profitability

This single factor often determines airline success.

Seat capacity utilization (load factor) is the primary driver of airline profitability, making revenue management and network optimization critical strategic priorities.

See Simple Flying →

Military Mar 27, 2026

[Updated] Iranian Attack on Prince Sultan Air Base Reportedly Struck U.S. KC-135s, E-3 Sentry

Satellite images show signs of an attack at Prince Sultan Air Base, Saudi Arabia, with The Wall Street Journal now reporting that an Iranian missile damaged “several U.S. refueling aircraft.” Satellite images emerged on Mar. 27, 2026, appeared to show signs of damage on an apron used by U.S....

Damage to KC-135 tankers reduces aerial refueling capacity across Middle East operations, directly impacting extended-range mission planning and coalition air operations.

See The Aviationist →

Commercial Mar 27, 2026

Southwest Airlines Adds 42 New Nonstop Routes This Spring [Map]

They include four new cities, with one in particular being historic...

Southwest's expansion into four new markets signals confidence in point-to-point demand recovery while 42 nonstop routes demonstrate their strategy to compete with legacy carriers on network density rather than hub consolidation.

See Simple Flying →

General Aviation Mar 28, 2026

From the Flight Deck – Eastern Iowa Airport, IA (CID)

Eastern Iowa Airport (CID) is a medium-sized multi-use airport located south of Cedar Rapids, Iowa. Traffic includes basic training aircraft up through air carrier aircraft. Flight schools often use...

Regional airports like CID diversify revenue by accommodating flight training, air cargo, and commercial carriers simultaneously, creating resilient operations beyond traditional passenger traffic.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Commercial Mar 27, 2026

“AI Can Help”: American Airlines’ Newest Solution To Get Rid Of Contrails

How the airline is working to ensure that contrails become a thing of the past, and why this is important.

American Airlines is leveraging AI to optimize flight paths and reduce contrail formation, addressing a climate impact factor that persists even as aviation pursues carbon-neutral fuels.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

I Am Artemis: Erik Richards

Listen to this audio excerpt from Erik Richards, Near Space Network Mission Manager: For Erik Richards, supporting NASA’s first crewed Artemis mission to the Moon and back is the culmination of a career spent helping spacecraft communicate with Earth. Like many kids who grew up at the height of the...

Ground communication infrastructure expertise, refined across decades of spacecraft missions, is now critical to Artemis's success in establishing sustained lunar operations.

See NASA →

Commercial Mar 27, 2026

How GE Aerospace Engines Stack Up Against The Rolls-Royce Trent Series

Which will come out on top?

GE and Rolls-Royce's competing engine architectures reveal divergent strategies in fuel efficiency, maintenance costs, and supply chain resilience that operators must weigh beyond raw performance metrics.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA Selects Intuitive Machines to Deliver Artemis Science, Tech to Moon

NASA has awarded Intuitive Machines of Houston, $180.4 million to deliver NASA-funded science and technology to the lunar surface as part of the agency’s CLPS (Commercial Lunar Payload Services) initiative and Artemis program. This lunar delivery, which includes seven payloads — five of them NASA’s...

NASA's $180.4M contract with Intuitive Machines expands commercial partnerships for lunar logistics, reducing agency dependence on traditional contractors while advancing Artemis infrastructure.

See NASA →

▶
Safety Mar 28, 2026

RNO Mustang 9 Departure

Pilots departing from runways 17 left and right at Reno-Tahoe International need to pay special attention to the Mustang 9 Departure. It is extremely important that the procedure on the Mustang 9...

Reno-Tahoe's Mustang 9 Departure demands strict adherence to published procedures for runways 17L/R to ensure terrain and obstacle clearance in the Sierra Nevada approach environment.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA’s Environment and Energy “Blue Marble” Awards Categories

Category I: NASA Environmental Quality Award Recognizes excellence in environmental management and planning, including stewardship of natural and cultural resources. This category highlights achievements in compliance, conservation, remediation, communication, and environmental information...

NASA's environmental awards framework establishes measurable standards for aerospace organizations to integrate sustainability practices into operations beyond compliance requirements.

See NASA →

Safety Mar 27, 2026

EASA warns non-airworthy stolen engine parts could re-enter the market

The European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) is warning airlines, maintenance providers and parts buyers to be on…...

Stolen engine parts from Ukraine conflict zones may flood the supply chain, creating hidden safety risks that standard documentation checks cannot detect.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

Commercial Space Federation (CSF) Welcomes Two New Associate Members

March 27, 2026 – Washington, D.C.—The Commercial Space Federation (CSF) is pleased to welcome Astrolab and Zeno Power, two innovative companies advancing planetary mobility and reliable energy in extreme environments. […]...

CSF's expansion to include lunar mobility and advanced power systems suppliers signals growing industry momentum toward sustained extraterrestrial operations beyond launch services.

See SpaceNews →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

ESA to decide by June on Europe’s Gateway contributions

MUNICH — For more than a year, questions have swirled about Artemis changes and the Gateway’s role in U.S. lunar ambitions. Following NASA’s March 24 decision to halt work on Gateway, the lunar-orbiting station that had been intended to support astronauts before and after lunar surface missions,...

NASA's Gateway halt forces ESA to reassess European lunar infrastructure investment timing amid evolving Artemis program priorities.

See SpaceNews →

Industry Mar 28, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational efficiency improvements across airlines, airports, and flight planning can simultaneously address capacity constraints and environmental impact as air traffic demand continues growing.

See MDPI →

Military Mar 27, 2026

Gallery: Luke Days 2026

A glimpse back at last weekend's air show at Luke Air Force Base in Arizona.

Luke AFB's biennial airshow remains a critical recruitment and public engagement platform for the U.S. Air Force fighter pilot community.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NISAR’s View of Mount Rainier

Description This image captured by U.S.-Indian Earth satellite NISAR on Nov. 10, 2025, shows Washington’s Mount Rainier. The image is cropped from a much larger swath spanning the Pacific Northwest on a cloudy day; NISAR’s L-band SAR instrument is able to peer through the clouds at the surface...

NISAR's L-band synthetic aperture radar enables all-weather Earth observation critical for monitoring volcanic hazards and infrastructure risks in regions with persistent cloud cover.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NISAR Views Mount St. Helens

Description This image captured by U.S.-Indian Earth satellite NISAR on Nov. 10, 2025, shows Washington’s Mount St. Helens. The image is cropped from a much larger swath spanning the Pacific Northwest on a cloudy day; NISAR’s L-band SAR instrument is able to peer through the clouds at the surface...

NISAR's synthetic aperture radar capability to penetrate cloud cover enables persistent Earth observation for disaster monitoring and volcanic risk assessment regardless of weather conditions.

See NASA →

Safety Mar 27, 2026

NTSB Issues Preliminary Report on Hudson River Ditching

Investigators say an instructional flight ended after a loss of oil pressure and total engine failure.

Oil pressure loss preceded the total engine failure, suggesting potential maintenance or mechanical degradation issues warrant examination across similar aircraft types.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA Tech and Science Bound for Low Earth Orbit on Commercial Launch

Technology and science demonstrations, supported by various NASA industry collaborations and agency developments, are set to launch to low Earth orbit aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket as part of the company’s Transporter-16 commercial rideshare mission. These demonstrations will test thermal...

NASA's increasing reliance on commercial rideshare missions reduces dedicated launch costs while enabling rapid iteration of experimental technologies in orbit.

See NASA →

Regulatory Mar 27, 2026

House Transportation Chair Sam Graves To Retire

Graves helped shape FAA reauthorization, BasicMed and several other general aviation measures.

Loss of a key FAA reauthorization architect may slow momentum on pending general aviation regulatory reforms and BasicMed expansions.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA Names Scientists to Support Lunar South Pole Science

NASA has selected 10 participating scientists to help shape a science plan for astronauts to complete on the lunar surface under the Artemis program – including deploying scientific instruments, making critical observations of the landing site, and collecting Moon rocks. “Congratulations to the...

NASA's selection of field scientists for Artemis lunar missions establishes a precedent for integrating scientific expertise into crewed spaceflight operations that could reshape how exploration missions are planned and executed.

See NASA →

Safety Mar 27, 2026

Three killed in Hawaii tour helicopter crash on Kauai

Three people died and two others were injured after an Airborne Aviation tour helicopter crashed on the afternoon…...

Hawaii's tour helicopter industry faces renewed scrutiny following another fatal accident, raising questions about operational oversight in a sector with a documented history of safety incidents.

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

I Am Artemis: Michael Guzman

Listen to this audio excerpt from Michael Guzman, Artemis II main propulsion systems engineer: A clue to what Mike Guzman, main propulsion systems engineer at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida, loves most can be found in the signature of his work email: a complex string of equations for rocket...

NASA's Artemis propulsion engineers are developing critical expertise in deep-space engine systems that will inform next-generation commercial aerospace propulsion technologies.

See NASA →

Commercial Mar 27, 2026

SES meoSphere will be core to new MEO/LEO inflight connectivity

WASHINGTON D.C. — Rapidly putting the wheels in motion to launch a next-generation Medium Earth Orbit satellite network, dubbed meoSphere, SES intends to pair the new service with Low Earth Orbit satcom to power multi-orbit MEO/LEO inflight connectivity for airlines, company CEO Adel Al-Saleh...

Airlines can expect hybrid MEO/LEO satellite networks to deliver more reliable, lower-latency inflight connectivity than single-orbit systems.

See Runway Girl Network →
